CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through method (MDP/Q‑learning formulation) to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (DRL‑Sense framework, joint training, non‑parametric sense discovery) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and the methodology description (sense selection vs. representation modules, reward‑passing) is understandable from the summary, and figures/tables seem to support evaluation on SCWS and baselines. A few presentation rough spots remain: the discussion of the policy‑gradient limitation is dense and could benefit from a concise intuitive example, and the lack of any note about code or public artifacts reduces reproducibility clarity. Overall the writing is good with only minor clarity issues. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (DRL-Sense, the two-module design, Q-learning joint training and non-parametric sense discovery). Notation and methodological description are clear enough to convey the MDP/Q-learning formulation and the reward-passing training; hyperparameters and related work are reported thoroughly. The writing quality is good, though from the summary there may be minor presentation issues (e.g., uncertain coverage of figures/tables or illustrative examples) that keep this from being an exceptional (5) clarity rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through method (MDP/Q‑learning formulation) to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (DRL‑Sense framework, joint training, non‑parametric sense discovery) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and the methodology description (sense selection vs. representation modules, reward‑passing) is understandable from the summary, and figures/tables seem to support evaluation on SCWS and baselines. A few presentation rough spots remain: the discussion of the policy‑gradient limitation is dense and could benefit from a concise intuitive example, and the lack of any note about code or public artifacts reduces reproducibility clarity. Overall the writing is good with only minor clarity issues.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (DRL-Sense, the two-module design, Q-learning joint training and non-parametric sense discovery). Notation and methodological description are clear enough to convey the MDP/Q-learning formulation and the reward-passing training; hyperparameters and related work are reported thoroughly. The writing quality is good, though from the summary there may be minor presentation issues (e.g., uncertain coverage of figures/tables or illustrative examples) that keep this from being an exceptional (5) clarity rating.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (multi-sense word representations) using a novel RL-based formulation and evaluates on standard benchmarks (SCWS) with appropriate metrics (Spearman ρ, MaxSimC, AvgSimC) and relevant baselines, which makes it well within scope for a top venue. The methodology (Q-learning sense selection + skip-gram sense representations), non-parametric sense discovery claim, and emphasis on efficiency directly target the conference audience of representation learning and NLP. From a reproducibility standpoint I note missing code release in the summary, but that does not detract from topical fit—rather it is a post-acceptance concern to require public code and exact preprocessing/training details. | B(5): This paper targets a core NLP/ML problem—multi-sense word representation—and proposes a novel RL-based joint training formulation with clear algorithmic contributions (linear-time sense selection, non-parametric sense induction) that are directly relevant to the community. The evaluation uses accepted benchmarks and metrics (SCWS, Spearman ρ, MaxSimC/AvgSimC) and reports strong empirical results, so the work will interest both representation-learning and semantic-lexicon researchers. Overall it sits squarely within the scope and audience of a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (multi-sense word representations) using a novel RL-based formulation and evaluates on standard benchmarks (SCWS) with appropriate metrics (Spearman ρ, MaxSimC, AvgSimC) and relevant baselines, which makes it well within scope for a top venue. The methodology (Q-learning sense selection + skip-gram sense representations), non-parametric sense discovery claim, and emphasis on efficiency directly target the conference audience of representation learning and NLP. From a reproducibility standpoint I note missing code release in the summary, but that does not detract from topical fit—rather it is a post-acceptance concern to require public code and exact preprocessing/training details.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper targets a core NLP/ML problem—multi-sense word representation—and proposes a novel RL-based joint training formulation with clear algorithmic contributions (linear-time sense selection, non-parametric sense induction) that are directly relevant to the community. The evaluation uses accepted benchmarks and metrics (SCWS, Spearman ρ, MaxSimC/AvgSimC) and reports strong empirical results, so the work will interest both representation-learning and semantic-lexicon researchers. Overall it sits squarely within the scope and audience of a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a notable and distinguishable idea: framing sense selection as an MDP and using a Q-learning/reward-passing scheme to propagate statistical information from sense representations into a selection policy. This RL-based joint training and the claim of efficient (linear-time) sense selection and non-parametric sense discovery materially differ from prior mixture/adaptive skip-gram and multilingual multi-sense methods (e.g., Neelakantan et al., Adaptive Skip-gram, mixture-model approaches). While the goal of learning multi-sense embeddings is well-studied and some components (skip-gram style losses, sense induction objectives) are familiar, the reinforcement-learning formulation and surrogate reward mechanism constitute a non-trivial and informative extension that would likely surprise practitioners familiar with existing probabilistic/mixture approaches. | B(4): The paper contributes a notable novel angle by framing sense selection as an MDP and using a Q-learning / reward-passing loop to jointly train a selection policy and pure sense-level representations—this is a clear methodological departure from prior mixture models (e.g., Neelakantan et al., AdaGram/Broken Sticks) and multilingual or syntax-aware multi-sense approaches. The linear-time selection claim and the use of the representation module’s collocation likelihood as a surrogate reward are technically distinctive and well-differentiated from existing probabilistic mixture and nonparametric skip-gram extensions. That said, the overall objective—inducing multi-sense embeddings and nonparametric sense counts—addresses a well-studied problem and builds on many prior ingredients, so the contribution is notable but not a radical break from established lines of work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a notable and distinguishable idea: framing sense selection as an MDP and using a Q-learning/reward-passing scheme to propagate statistical information from sense representations into a selection policy. This RL-based joint training and the claim of efficient (linear-time) sense selection and non-parametric sense discovery materially differ from prior mixture/adaptive skip-gram and multilingual multi-sense methods (e.g., Neelakantan et al., Adaptive Skip-gram, mixture-model approaches). While the goal of learning multi-sense embeddings is well-studied and some components (skip-gram style losses, sense induction objectives) are familiar, the reinforcement-learning formulation and surrogate reward mechanism constitute a non-trivial and informative extension that would likely surprise practitioners familiar with existing probabilistic/mixture approaches.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper contributes a notable novel angle by framing sense selection as an MDP and using a Q-learning / reward-passing loop to jointly train a selection policy and pure sense-level representations—this is a clear methodological departure from prior mixture models (e.g., Neelakantan et al., AdaGram/Broken Sticks) and multilingual or syntax-aware multi-sense approaches. The linear-time selection claim and the use of the representation module’s collocation likelihood as a surrogate reward are technically distinctive and well-differentiated from existing probabilistic mixture and nonparametric skip-gram extensions. That said, the overall objective—inducing multi-sense embeddings and nonparametric sense counts—addresses a well-studied problem and builds on many prior ingredients, so the contribution is notable but not a radical break from established lines of work.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents a coherent RL formulation and joint training procedure and compares against appropriate baselines on SCWS with reported metrics, but several methodological gaps weaken full technical confidence. The summary notes known optimization issues with the joint probabilistic (policy-gradient) formulation and an unexplained claim of linear-time sense selection; statistical significance, wall-clock/runtime and memory measurements, and release of code/data are not reported, limiting reproducibility. While hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough, the non-parametric sense-count discovery (≈30k polysemous words) and some optimization assertions require additional validation (ablation, seed sensitivity, and complexity analysis) to fully support the claims. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and (per the summary) supported by empirical results against reasonable baselines on SCWS and a large Wikipedia corpus, and the RL/Q-learning formulation with a reward-passing training loop is described and implemented. However, the summary notes a known limitation in the policy-gradient joint probabilistic formulation that can under-estimate unseen senses, which indicates an unresolved optimization caveat; moreover no mention is made of statistical significance testing or formal proofs of convergence for the Q-learning procedure. Overall the methodology and reported experiments appear sound, but the optimization concern and lack of certain theoretical/ statistical details justify a minor deduction.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a coherent RL formulation and joint training procedure and compares against appropriate baselines on SCWS with reported metrics, but several methodological gaps weaken full technical confidence. The summary notes known optimization issues with the joint probabilistic (policy-gradient) formulation and an unexplained claim of linear-time sense selection; statistical significance, wall-clock/runtime and memory measurements, and release of code/data are not reported, limiting reproducibility. While hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough, the non-parametric sense-count discovery (≈30k polysemous words) and some optimization assertions require additional validation (ablation, seed sensitivity, and complexity analysis) to fully support the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and (per the summary) supported by empirical results against reasonable baselines on SCWS and a large Wikipedia corpus, and the RL/Q-learning formulation with a reward-passing training loop is described and implemented. However, the summary notes a known limitation in the policy-gradient joint probabilistic formulation that can under-estimate unseen senses, which indicates an unresolved optimization caveat; moreover no mention is made of statistical significance testing or formal proofs of convergence for the Q-learning procedure. Overall the methodology and reported experiments appear sound, but the optimization concern and lack of certain theoretical/ statistical details justify a minor deduction.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a promising DRL-based joint training formulation and reports strong SCWS performance and a large-scale non-parametric sense discovery (~30k polysemous words), and it compares against appropriate baselines (Neelakantan et al., Bartunov et al.). However, from a reproducibility standpoint key elements are missing: code/data release is not mentioned, ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., seed variance, hyperparameter sweeps, effect of the reward-passing design) and wall-clock/complexity measurements for the linear-time claim are absent. Related work appears thoroughly covered, and some limitations are discussed, but additional failure-case analysis and concrete reproducibility artifacts are required to fully support the claims. | B(3): The paper presents a technically plausible DRL formulation, compares to a broad set of prior multi-sense methods, reports strong intrinsic results on SCWS and an impressive non-parametric sense discovery (≈30k polysemous words), and discusses a key limitation of the policy-gradient alternative. However, evaluation is narrowly focused on intrinsic metrics (SCWS/MaxSimC/AvgSimC) with no downstream task evidence of utility or explicit ablation/sensitivity studies reported for the reward-passing/Q-learning choices; reproducibility is partially supported (Wikipedia dataset and hyperparameters) but code availability is not mentioned. Given thorough related-work coverage and discussion of limitations but missing critical analyses (ablations, downstream impact), I rate the experimental thoroughness as adequate but incomplete.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a promising DRL-based joint training formulation and reports strong SCWS performance and a large-scale non-parametric sense discovery (~30k polysemous words), and it compares against appropriate baselines (Neelakantan et al., Bartunov et al.). However, from a reproducibility standpoint key elements are missing: code/data release is not mentioned, ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., seed variance, hyperparameter sweeps, effect of the reward-passing design) and wall-clock/complexity measurements for the linear-time claim are absent. Related work appears thoroughly covered, and some limitations are discussed, but additional failure-case analysis and concrete reproducibility artifacts are required to fully support the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a technically plausible DRL formulation, compares to a broad set of prior multi-sense methods, reports strong intrinsic results on SCWS and an impressive non-parametric sense discovery (≈30k polysemous words), and discusses a key limitation of the policy-gradient alternative. However, evaluation is narrowly focused on intrinsic metrics (SCWS/MaxSimC/AvgSimC) with no downstream task evidence of utility or explicit ablation/sensitivity studies reported for the reward-passing/Q-learning choices; reproducibility is partially supported (Wikipedia dataset and hyperparameters) but code availability is not mentioned. Given thorough related-work coverage and discussion of limitations but missing critical analyses (ablations, downstream impact), I rate the experimental thoroughness as adequate but incomplete.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses the important problem of efficient, multi-sense word representation learning by introducing a reinforcement-learning formulation that jointly trains sense selection and sense representations and claims linear-time selection while supporting non-parametric sense discovery (~30k polysemous words). Its reported state-of-the-art on SCWS and data-efficiency (word2vec parity with 1/100 training data) indicate the approach could change how researchers design sense induction and selection mechanisms and encourage more RL-based, joint-training strategies. While not necessarily transformative—established embedding pipelines remain strong—the combination of efficiency, joint optimization, and automatic sense-count discovery makes it a strong contribution likely to be adopted and extended by others. | B(4): The paper tackles an important and long-standing problem—efficiently learning multi-sense word representations—and introduces a practically appealing RL-based framework (DRL-Sense) that enables linear-time sense selection while jointly training sense representations. Empirical claims of SOTA on SCWS, discovery of ~30k polysemous words via non-parametric sensing, and strong data-efficiency (comparable to word2vec with 1/100 training data) suggest the approach could materially influence how researchers design sense induction and scalable representation models. The work plausibly opens new directions (reinforcement learning for sense selection, reward-passing training) and is likely to be built upon, though its broader impact on downstream tasks beyond contextual similarity remains to be demonstrated.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses the important problem of efficient, multi-sense word representation learning by introducing a reinforcement-learning formulation that jointly trains sense selection and sense representations and claims linear-time selection while supporting non-parametric sense discovery (~30k polysemous words). Its reported state-of-the-art on SCWS and data-efficiency (word2vec parity with 1/100 training data) indicate the approach could change how researchers design sense induction and selection mechanisms and encourage more RL-based, joint-training strategies. While not necessarily transformative—established embedding pipelines remain strong—the combination of efficiency, joint optimization, and automatic sense-count discovery makes it a strong contribution likely to be adopted and extended by others.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important and long-standing problem—efficiently learning multi-sense word representations—and introduces a practically appealing RL-based framework (DRL-Sense) that enables linear-time sense selection while jointly training sense representations. Empirical claims of SOTA on SCWS, discovery of ~30k polysemous words via non-parametric sensing, and strong data-efficiency (comparable to word2vec with 1/100 training data) suggest the approach could materially influence how researchers design sense induction and scalable representation models. The work plausibly opens new directions (reinforcement learning for sense selection, reward-passing training) and is likely to be built upon, though its broader impact on downstream tasks beyond contextual similarity remains to be demonstrated.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the major and canonical multi-sense baselines (Huang et al. 2012; Neelakantan et al. 2014; Tian et al. 2014; Bartunov et al. 2016/AdaGram; Qiu et al. 2016; Li & Jurafsky 2015) and evaluates on the standard SCWS benchmark with Spearman/MaxSimC/AvgSimC, which supports fair head-to-head comparisons. The authors also describe architectural and objective differences (Q-learning sense selection vs. prior mixture or adaptive-skipgram approaches) and report where their method achieves SOTA or comparable performance. Minor gaps remain: a few relevant later mixture-model and multilingual multi-sense works (e.g., 2017 mixture-model papers) do not appear in the experimental baselines, and the manuscript could be clearer about acknowledging any baselines that match or outperform DRL-Sense across all metrics. Overall the comparisons are solid and reproducible but not exhaustively comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares against a solid set of established multi-sense baselines (Huang et al. 2012; Neelakantan et al. 2014; Tian et al. 2014; Bartunov et al. 2016; Qiu et al. 2016; Li & Jurafsky 2015) and evaluates on standard data (SCWS) with standard metrics (Spearman ρ, MaxSimC, AvgSimC), so comparisons appear generally fair. However, the literature survey found highly relevant recent mixture-model and multilingual multi-sense papers from 2017 that are not listed among baselines, so the empirical comparison omits some important contemporaneous approaches. The manuscript does discuss limitations of alternative joint probabilistic formulations and reports when others are comparable, but it could more directly analyze differences versus the omitted 2017 mixture/adaptive-sense methods and show any head-to-head deficits. Overall the comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive given recent relevant work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against the major and canonical multi-sense baselines (Huang et al. 2012; Neelakantan et al. 2014; Tian et al. 2014; Bartunov et al. 2016/AdaGram; Qiu et al. 2016; Li & Jurafsky 2015) and evaluates on the standard SCWS benchmark with Spearman/MaxSimC/AvgSimC, which supports fair head-to-head comparisons. The authors also describe architectural and objective differences (Q-learning sense selection vs. prior mixture or adaptive-skipgram approaches) and report where their method achieves SOTA or comparable performance. Minor gaps remain: a few relevant later mixture-model and multilingual multi-sense works (e.g., 2017 mixture-model papers) do not appear in the experimental baselines, and the manuscript could be clearer about acknowledging any baselines that match or outperform DRL-Sense across all metrics. Overall the comparisons are solid and reproducible but not exhaustively comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a solid set of established multi-sense baselines (Huang et al. 2012; Neelakantan et al. 2014; Tian et al. 2014; Bartunov et al. 2016; Qiu et al. 2016; Li & Jurafsky 2015) and evaluates on standard data (SCWS) with standard metrics (Spearman ρ, MaxSimC, AvgSimC), so comparisons appear generally fair. However, the literature survey found highly relevant recent mixture-model and multilingual multi-sense papers from 2017 that are not listed among baselines, so the empirical comparison omits some important contemporaneous approaches. The manuscript does discuss limitations of alternative joint probabilistic formulations and reports when others are comparable, but it could more directly analyze differences versus the omitted 2017 mixture/adaptive-sense methods and show any head-to-head deficits. Overall the comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive given recent relevant work.

main_claims: Proposes DRL-Sense, a reinforcement learning based framework for learning multi-sense word representations composed of a sense selection module and a sense representation module.

Jointly trains sense selection and sense representation modules via a Q-learning formulation with a reward-passing procedure, enabling efficient (linear-time) sense selection while maintaining pure sense-level representation learning.
Supports non-parametric learning to automatically decide the number of senses per word and demonstrates effective sense induction (reports ~30k polysemous words discovered).
Reports state-of-the-art performance on contextual word similarity (SCWS) and comparable performance to word2vec using only 1/100 of training data.
_summary.methodology: Formulates sense selection as an MDP and uses a Q-learning approach: a sense selection module (computing Q-values via a CBOW-style context sum and a sigmoid) selects senses, which are passed to a sense representation module (skip-gram with negative sampling) whose collocation likelihood is used as a reward to train the selection module; modules are trained jointly with a cross-entropy loss.
limitations: Policy-gradient joint probabilistic formulation requires fitting a valid probability distribution and can underestimate probabilities of senses not yet selected during optimization.
Using stochastic gradient ascent on the joint log-likelihood (logCo ≤ 0) can decrease the estimated probability for selected senses (undesirable when selections are correct).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - multi-sense word representations / word embeddings

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methods Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a decade of experience building, evaluating, and deploying NLP models; has led reproducibility audits and benchmark suites for word-embedding and representation learning research.
traits: insists on clear baselines, exact training/configuration details, and public release of code and preprocessed data
looks for rigorous experimental controls: ablations, hyperparameter sweeps, and statistical significance reporting
checks computational cost claims carefully (time, memory, dataset size) and values wall-clock and complexity measurements
evaluates reproducibility of non-parametric mechanisms (e.g., sense-count discovery) and sensitivity to random seeds
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on methodological soundness and reproducibility: verifies that experiments fairly support the claims, requests missing details or experiments, and prescribes concrete steps to make results verifiable.

reviewer_b.name: Lexical Semantics Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Academic researcher in lexical semantics and distributional models with extensive publications on multi-sense embeddings, sense induction, and evaluation protocols for contextual representations.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior multi-sense and contextual embedding work and notices subtle technical parallels to past methods
values meaningful linguistic evaluation (sense induction accuracy, interpretability, downstream impact) in addition to intrinsic metrics
examines whether proposed advances address real gaps in the literature (scalability, interpretability, integration with downstream tasks)
considers broader impact: utility for downstream NLP tasks, handling of rare senses, and implications of automatic sense proliferation
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates conceptual novelty and impact: situates the work within the literature, judges whether the technical contributions advance understanding or practice, and recommends additional analyses to demonstrate linguistic and downstream relevance.
